summary:
Patients with Special Challenges

As an EMS provider, you will encounter patients with a wide range of physical, developmental, cognitive, and psychosocial challenges. Providing competent and compassionate care to these patients requires awareness of their unique needs, effective communication strategies, and adaptations to your standard assessment and treatment approach. The fundamental principle: treat the medical emergency first, even in patients with behavioral or developmental conditions. A change in behavior may be a sign of an underlying medical problem.

Patients with Developmental/Intellectual Disabilities

Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD)

Autism spectrum disorder is a neurodevelopmental condition characterized by challenges in social communication, restricted interests, and repetitive behaviors. The prevalence of ASD is approximately 1 in 36 children, and many of these children grow up to be autistic adults. Key considerations for EMS:

  • Communication challenges: The patient may be non-verbal, have delayed language, or use alternative communication methods (picture boards, tablets, sign language). Ask the caregiver how the patient communicates. Speak in clear, simple sentences. Allow extra processing time — do not rush the patient.
  • Sensory sensitivities: Many autistic individuals have hypersensitivity to light, sound, touch, and smells. The EMS environment (sirens, radios, bright lights, cold stethoscope, multiple people touching them) can cause extreme distress. Reduce stimulation where possible: turn down lights, minimize unnecessary equipment noise, reduce the number of people interacting with the patient.
  • Consider weighted blankets or deep pressure: Some autistic patients find deep pressure calming. If the patient is in distress, a weighted blanket (if available) or firm, gentle touch on the shoulders may help.
  • Predictability: Explain every step before you do it. Use a calm, monotone voice. Avoid unexpected touches. Give the patient a sense of control when possible.
  • Do NOT assume inability to understand: Many autistic individuals understand more than they can express. Talk to them directly, not just to the caregiver.
  • Pain can present as behavioral escalation: An autistic patient who is aggressive, screaming, or self-injuring may be in pain or medical distress, not "acting out." Medical causes should always be considered.
  • Wandering/elopement risk: Autistic children (and some adults) may wander away from safe environments. If you encounter an autistic patient who has wandered, assess for injury and exposure.
Down Syndrome (Trisomy 21)

Down syndrome is the most common chromosomal disorder, occurring in approximately 1 in 700 births. Patients with Down syndrome have specific anatomical and medical considerations:

  • Atlantoaxial instability: Approximately 10-30% of individuals with Down syndrome have instability at the C1-C2 junction (atlantoaxial joint). This means the neck is at increased risk of spinal cord injury from hyperextension or flexion. Maintain cervical spine precautions during any trauma assessment — use a cervical collar and keep the head in neutral alignment. Be especially careful during airway management and positioning.
  • Congenital heart defects: Approximately 50% of children with Down syndrome are born with congenital heart disease (most commonly atrioventricular septal defect, VSD, or tetralogy of Fallot). These patients may have baseline cyanosis, clubbing, or reduced exercise tolerance. Be alert for signs of heart failure or hypoxia.
  • Smaller airway: Individuals with Down syndrome have a smaller trachea, a larger tongue (macroglossia), and smaller nasal passages. This makes airway management more challenging — BVM ventilation may require a modified technique, and suction should be readily available.
  • Hypotonia: Decreased muscle tone affects the airway (increased risk of obstruction), GI motility, and overall motor function. The patient may have a weak cough and increased risk of aspiration.
  • Higher pain tolerance? There is some evidence that individuals with Down syndrome may have altered pain perception. "Silent" abdominal emergencies (e.g., appendicitis without typical pain) have been reported — maintain a high index of suspicion.
  • Obesity and hypothyroidism: Both are common in Down syndrome and contribute to additional health challenges.
Clinical Pearl: When assessing a patient with Down syndrome who has a trauma mechanism (even minor), maintain cervical spine precautions due to the risk of atlantoaxial instability. Also be prepared for a difficult airway — the combination of macroglossia, small trachea, and hypotonia can make BVM and intubation challenging.

Hearing and Vision Impairment

Communication with patients who are deaf, hard of hearing, or blind requires specific adaptations:

ImpairmentCommunication TechniquesAvoid
Hearing Impairment / DeafnessFace the patient directly (lip-reading), ensure good lighting, use written aids (pen and paper, phone screen), use simple gestures, learn basic ASL signs if possible, use a sign language interpreter (in-person or video relay), speak in a normal tone (do not shout)Shouting (distorts lip-reading and is ineffective), speaking with your back turned or in poor lighting, assuming the patient cannot communicate at all, using family members (especially children) as interpreters
Vision Impairment / BlindnessIdentify yourself clearly by name and role when you enter, explain everything you are doing before you touch the patient, orient them to the environment, offer your arm for guidance, describe the surroundings, warn about steps or obstacles, use tactile cues, maintain verbal contactGrabbing the patient without warning, assuming they need help walking (ask first), using visual references ("over there," "this"), leaving without saying you are leaving, rearranging their environment without telling them

Patients with Mental Illness

Mental illness affects millions of Americans, and EMS providers frequently interact with patients experiencing psychiatric crises. The management of these patients requires a balance of compassion, de-escalation, and medical vigilance:

  • De-escalation techniques: Maintain a calm, respectful tone. Use the patient's name. Acknowledge their feelings: "I can see you're very upset. I'm here to help you." Give clear, simple choices. Avoid arguing, threatening, or challenging delusions. Maintain a safe distance and keep an exit path clear.
  • Medical causes first — always: Behavioral is not always behavioral. A patient with new-onset psychosis, agitation, or altered behavior may have an underlying medical cause: hypoglycemia, hypoxia, infection (especially UTI in older adults), head injury, intoxication, withdrawal, medication side effect, stroke, or seizure. Assess vital signs, check blood glucose, and look for focal neurological signs before assuming a purely psychiatric cause.
  • Suicide assessment: Ask directly about suicidal thoughts. Research shows that asking about suicide does NOT increase the risk — it reduces it. Use the mnemonic: "Have you had thoughts of hurting yourself or ending your life? Do you have a plan? Do you have the means to carry it out?" If the patient answers yes to all three, they are at high risk and should not be left alone. Involve law enforcement for a mental health hold if needed.
  • Psychiatric holds: Patients who are a danger to themselves or others due to mental illness may be placed on an involuntary psychiatric hold (varies by state). These patients cannot refuse transport. The EMS provider transports under the legal authority of the hold; law enforcement typically manages security and restraint decisions.
  • Medication non-compliance: Many psychiatric patients have stopped taking their medications. Common withdrawal syndromes and relapse presentations include: antipsychotics (return of psychotic symptoms), antidepressants (mood relapse, discontinuation syndrome), mood stabilizers (mania or depression recurrence including lithium toxicity if dehydrated), and anxiolytics (anxiety recurrence, withdrawal seizures from benzodiazepines).
  • Restraint considerations: If restraints are needed, they should be applied by law enforcement, not EMS, unless your protocols specifically allow it. If you must restrain, use the least restrictive method necessary. Monitor the restrained patient closely for respiratory compromise (positional asphyxia) — do not place the patient face-down, and monitor vital signs frequently.
NREMT Key Point: A patient with a known psychiatric history who presents with ACUTE agitation or confusion has a medical problem until proven otherwise. Hypoglycemia, hypoxia, intoxication, withdrawal, infection, and head injury can all present as "psychiatric" symptoms. Check vital signs and blood glucose on EVERY patient with altered behavior. Medical emergencies take priority over behavioral crises.

Patients Who Are Homeless

Individuals experiencing homelessness face significant barriers to healthcare and have higher rates of both acute and chronic illness. EMS may be their only contact with the healthcare system:

  • Exposure-related illness: Hypothermia (even in moderate cold — compounded by wet clothing, alcohol use, malnutrition), hyperthermia/heat stroke, frostbite, and immersion foot (trench foot).
  • Malnutrition and dehydration: Poor nutritional intake leads to electrolyte abnormalities, vitamin deficiencies (Wernicke-Korsakoff from thiamine deficiency in alcoholism), and weakened immune response.
  • Infectious diseases: Higher rates of tuberculosis, hepatitis (B and C), HIV, cellulitis (from minor wounds that go untreated), pneumonia, and infestations (lice, scabies).
  • Skin and wound issues: Poor wound healing, infected ulcers, diabetic foot infections, frostbite, and pressure injuries from sleeping on hard surfaces.
  • Lack of follow-up: Homeless patients may be discharged from the hospital before fully recovered, or may be unable to fill prescriptions, keep follow-up appointments, or adhere to treatment plans. Consider this in your transport decision and report to the receiving facility.
  • Trauma: Higher rates of both intentional (assault, robbery) and unintentional (being struck by vehicles, falls) trauma.
  • Approach: Treat all patients with dignity and respect. Do not judge. Address the patient using their name. Ask about their immediate needs — not just the medical complaint. Be aware that homeless patients may avoid hospitals due to past negative experiences. Explain why transport is important and what will happen at the hospital.

Non-English Speaking Patients

Language barriers are a significant challenge in EMS and a known contributor to medical errors:

  • Professional interpreter services: Use a certified medical interpreter whenever possible. Most dispatch centers and hospitals have access to over-the-phone interpretation services in hundreds of languages. These services are fast, accurate, and available 24/7. Do NOT delay critical care while waiting for an interpreter — use simple English or gestures for immediate concerns and activate the interpreter for the detailed assessment.
  • Family interpreters — caution: Avoid using family members as interpreters, especially children. Family members may: filter information, add their own opinions, omit sensitive details, or be biased. A child should never interpret for a parent in a medical setting — it is inappropriate and can cause role reversal and emotional harm.
  • Simple communication aids: Learn a few key phrases in the most common languages in your area. Use translation apps on your phone. Use picture charts and pain scales. Speak slowly and clearly — not loudly.
  • Speak to the patient, not the interpreter: When using an interpreter, maintain eye contact with the patient. Address the patient directly. The interpreter is a conduit, not a separate conversation partner.

Patients with Chronic Medical Conditions and Devices

You will increasingly encounter patients living at home with medical devices that were once only found in hospitals. Understanding these devices is critical to providing safe care:

Device/ConditionKey Considerations
Ventilator-dependentIf the ventilator alarms, the patient needs attention. Take your own ventilator if available for transport. If the patient has a tracheostomy, you may need to suction or manually ventilate via the trach. Know how to disconnect and reconnect the ventilator circuit. Bring the ventilator and all backup equipment. If the ventilator fails, manually ventilate with a BVM connected to the trach tube.
TracheostomySuction the trach if you hear gurgling or see secretions. For BVM ventilation, use a pediatric mask over the trach or a trach tube adapter. If you cannot ventilate via the trach, the tube may be blocked — attempt to suction or replace. Always carry a suction unit.
Feeding tubes (NG, PEG, G-tube, J-tube)Do NOT pull on or remove feeding tubes. If the tube is bleeding or dislodged, do not attempt to reinsert — transport for evaluation. If the patient has a PEG (directly into stomach), it may have a balloon that can be replaced. Document tube type and any problems.
Dialysis access (AV fistula, graft, catheter)NEVER take blood pressure, start an IV, or draw blood from the arm with an AV fistula or graft — this can damage the access site and cause thrombosis. Use the other arm. Look for signs of infection (redness, warmth, purulence) at the access site. Dialysis patients are at high risk for fluid and electrolyte emergencies.
Ostomies (colostomy, ileostomy, urostomy)Ostomies are surgical openings for elimination. They have no nerve endings — they are not painful to touch. If the ostomy is bleeding or has prolapsed tissue, cover with a moist dressing and transport. Document stoma color (should be pink/red — pale or blue indicates ischemia).
Shunts (VP shunt for hydrocephalus)A ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt drains CSF from the brain into the abdomen. Shunt malfunction presents with headache, vomiting, altered mental status, or seizures. The shunt tubing may be palpable under the skin behind the ear and down the neck. Do not put pressure on the shunt tubing.

Obesity (see also Section 7.8 — Bariatric Considerations)

Obesity presents unique challenges in patient assessment and management. Key considerations include:

  • Transport challenges: Bariatric stretchers, extra personnel for lifting, wide-door ambulances, mechanical lifts. Plan ahead — call for additional resources early.
  • Altered drug distribution: Lipophilic drugs distribute differently in adipose tissue. The volume of distribution is increased for some drugs (benzodiazepines, opiates) and unchanged for others.
  • Sleep apnea: Common in obesity. These patients may have chronic hypoxemia and are at higher risk of airway obstruction during sedation or positioning.
  • Difficult airway: Limited neck mobility, redundant pharyngeal tissue, large tongue, and decreased functional residual capacity make airway management challenging. Use the ramped position (see Section 7.8).
  • Proper lifting techniques: Use mechanical lifts when available. If lifting manually, use proper body mechanics — lift with your legs, not your back. Request sufficient personnel (4-6+ people) before attempting to lift or move a bariatric patient.
Clinical Pearl: When caring for any patient with special challenges, remember the "treat first" principle. Whether the patient has autism, dementia, a psychiatric condition, or a developmental disability, if they present with an acute change in status, assume there is a medical cause until proven otherwise. Always check vital signs and blood glucose. Always transport for evaluation if the cause is unclear.

quiz.q: An EMS provider is assessing a 5-year-old child with autism who is non-verbal. The child is crying, pulling at his ears, and hitting his head against the wall. What should the EMS provider consider FIRST?
options: The child is having a behavioral tantrum — use restraint techniques
The child may be in pain from an ear infection or other medical cause — assess for fever, injury, and other signs of illness
The child needs sedation — transport emergently for psychiatric evaluation
This is normal autistic behavior — no intervention is needed

quiz.q: A 35-year-old male with known schizophrenia presents with acute agitation and confusion. His vital signs: BP 88/56, HR 118, RR 24, SpO₂ 91%, blood glucose 42 mg/dL. What is the MOST likely cause of his agitation?
options: Acute psychotic episode
Hypoglycemia — the low blood glucose is causing altered mental status that looks psychiatric
A panic attack
His schizophrenia medications are not working

quiz.q: A 42-year-old woman with Down syndrome is involved in a minor fender-bender. She has no visible injuries and is ambulatory. She has midline cervical spine tenderness. What is the EMS provider's priority concern?
options: Down syndrome patients are always fine in minor accidents — clear her without immobilization
Atlantoaxial instability risk — apply a cervical collar and maintain spinal precautions despite the minor mechanism
She likely has a concussion — focus on the head injury assessment
She is just scared — reassurance is all that is needed

quiz.q: An EMS provider is caring for a patient who is blind. Which communication technique is MOST appropriate?
options: Take the patient's arm and guide them without speaking since they cannot see you
Identify yourself by name and role, explain what you are doing before touching, and provide verbal orientation to the environment
Speak loudly since blind people often have hearing loss
Avoid touching the patient since they cannot see what you are doing

quiz.q: A homeless patient is found sleeping on a park bench in 35°F weather. The patient is awake but confused, has cold, mottled skin, and is shivering. What should the EMS provider do?
options: Give the patient a warm blanket and let them sleep — they need rest
This is hypothermia — transport for evaluation and rewarming. Assess for concurrent conditions (malnutrition, infection, frostbite, alcohol use). Treat the patient with dignity
Call law enforcement for a wellness check since the patient is homeless
Give the patient hot coffee and have them walk around to warm up

quiz.q: An EMS provider needs to start an IV on a 60-year-old female who is a dialysis patient. She has an AV fistula in her left arm that is visibly palpable. Where should the EMS provider attempt IV access?
options: In the left arm — the fistula has strong blood flow so the IV will work well
In the right arm — NEVER use the arm with an AV fistula for IV, BP, or blood draws
In the left hand — it is below the fistula so it is safe
In the left arm above the fistula

quiz.q: HARD: An EMS provider is transporting a 22-year-old autistic male who is agitated and trying to pull off his oxygen mask. His mother, who is in the ambulance, says, 'He hates being touched and he's terrified of the hospital. He's not having a medical emergency — he's scared. Can you just let him calm down at home?' The patient's SpO₂ is 89% on room air, he has diffuse wheezing, and he has a history of asthma. What should the EMS provider do?
options: Respect the mother's judgment — autistic patients do better at home with familiar routines

Explain that the patient has hypoxia and wheezing from an asthma exacerbation — this IS a medical emergency. The agitation is likely from respiratory distress AND anxiety. Reduce stimuli (dim lights, minimize touching), explain every step, and continue oxygen and transport. Involve the mother in calming techniques she knows work
Restrain the patient so the oxygen mask stays on
Give the patient a sedative to calm him down so he tolerates the oxygen

quiz.q: HARD: A 30-year-old female who is deaf presents with abdominal pain. She uses American Sign Language (ASL). Her hearing mother is present and offers to interpret. What is the BEST approach?
options: Use the mother as the interpreter — she knows her daughter best and will be the most accurate

Use a certified ASL interpreter (in-person or video relay service). Avoid using family members as interpreters due to potential bias and filtering. If an interpreter is immediately available, use simple written communication and gestures in the meantime
Speak to the mother and have her tell the daughter what you said
Write your questions on paper — since the patient is deaf, she will understand written English perfectly

flashcards.question: What are the key EMS considerations for a patient with autism spectrum disorder?
flashcards.answer: Communication challenges (may be non-verbal or use AAC), sensory sensitivities (reduce light/sound/touch), use predictable step-by-step explanations, allow extra processing time, and consider weighted blankets for calming. Do NOT assume inability to understand.

flashcards.question: What is atlantoaxial instability in Down syndrome and why is it important for EMS?
flashcards.answer: Approximately 10-30% of Down syndrome patients have instability at C1-C2, increasing risk of spinal cord injury during neck movement. Maintain cervical spine precautions during trauma assessment and airway management. Be careful with head positioning.

flashcards.question: What are the key de-escalation techniques for managing a patient with acute mental illness?
flashcards.answer: Maintain calm, respectful tone. Use the patient's name. Acknowledge feelings. Give clear choices. Avoid arguing or challenging delusions. Maintain a safe distance with an exit path. If agitated, assess for medical causes first (hypoglycemia, hypoxia, intoxication).

flashcards.question: How should an EMS provider communicate with a patient who is deaf or hard of hearing?
flashcards.answer: Face the patient directly (lip-reading), ensure good lighting, use written aids (paper, phone screen), use gestures, use a sign language interpreter if available. Speak in a normal tone — do NOT shout. Do not use children as interpreters.

flashcards.question: When using an interpreter for a non-English speaking patient, what are the key communication principles?
flashcards.answer: Speak TO the patient, not the interpreter. Maintain eye contact with the patient. Use a professional medical interpreter — avoid using family members (especially children). Speak in short, clear sentences. Allow time for interpretation.

flashcards.question: Why should EMS providers avoid taking blood pressure in the arm with an AV fistula or graft?
flashcards.answer: Taking BP or starting an IV in an arm with an AV fistula or graft can damage the access site, cause thrombosis, and compromise future dialysis access. Always use the other arm for BP, IVs, and blood draws.

flashcards.question: What is the correct approach when an autistic patient in distress becomes aggressive or self-injurious?
flashcards.answer: Consider that the behavior may be a manifestation of pain or medical distress, not 'acting out.' Reduce environmental stimulation. Use calm, simple language. Assess for injury or illness. Ask caregivers about the patient's baseline and pain expression. Transport for evaluation if needed.

flashcards.question: What is the 'treat first' principle for patients with special challenges?
flashcards.answer: Always treat the medical emergency first, even in patients with behavioral health conditions. A change in behavior may be caused by an underlying medical problem (infection, hypoglycemia, hypoxia, head injury). Do not assume new behavioral changes are psychiatric. Check vital signs and blood glucose on every patient.

critical.scenario: You are dispatched to a group home for a 28-year-old male with autism and intellectual disability who is 'aggressive.' Staff report he has been 'having a bad day' and is hitting staff and banging his head against the wall. They say, 'He does this sometimes when he's upset about schedule changes. He just needs to be left alone.' You find the patient in his room, rocking back and forth, hitting his head with his fist, and shouting. A staff member is trying to restrain him. You notice the patient is flushed, his skin is warm, and he is breathing rapidly. When you ask the staff if he has any medical problems, they say he has a history of seizures. The patient cannot tell you what is wrong.
critical.question: Given this patient's autism, self-injurious behavior, flushing, tachypnea, and seizure history, what is the EMS provider's priority?
options: The staff knows him best — leave him to calm down in a quiet room as they recommend

The patient's agitation may be caused by a medical problem (post-ictal state, infection, pain, or medication side effect). Do not assume this is purely behavioral. Obtain vital signs, check blood glucose, look for signs of injury or illness. Reduce environmental stimulation. If the patient cannot be safely assessed or is at risk of harming himself, transport for medical evaluation. If he is post-ictal, request ALS
Restrain the patient immediately so he stops hitting his head
Call law enforcement to handle the behavioral situation before you approach

critical.scenario: You respond to a psychiatric outpatient clinic for a 45-year-old female who is 'acting strangely.' The receptionist reports that the patient came in for her appointment but is 'talking nonsense and seems confused.' The patient has a history of bipolar disorder and is on lithium. She is disheveled, has slurred speech, and is unsteady on her feet. She tells you she has been feeling 'worse' for about a week and has had diarrhea and vomiting for two days. One of the clinic nurses says, 'She's probably just having a manic episode — she's non-compliant with her medications all the time.' The patient's vital signs: BP 100/62, HR 108, RR 20, SpO₂ 97%, blood glucose 88 mg/dL.
critical.question: Given this patient's presentation (confusion, slurred speech, ataxia, GI symptoms) and history of lithium therapy, what is the most likely cause of her altered mental status?
options: Acute mania from stopping her lithium

Lithium toxicity — the triad of confusion, slurred speech/ataxia, and GI symptoms (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea) is classic for lithium toxicity. Dehydration from vomiting reduces the kidneys' ability to clear lithium, worsening the toxicity. This is a MEDICAL emergency, not a psychiatric one. Transport for evaluation of lithium levels and treatment
A panic attack
Alcohol intoxication

critical.scenario: You are called to a homeless encampment for a 55-year-old male with a 'leg wound.' You find the patient sitting on a mattress on the ground, with his pant leg rolled up. His right lower leg is swollen, red, warm, and has a foul-smelling, purulent wound approximately 4 cm in diameter on the anterior shin. The surrounding skin has blisters and dark discoloration extending up to the knee. The patient has a fever of 102.4°F, HR 112, and appears confused and lethargic. He has a strong smell of alcohol on his breath and empty liquor bottles nearby. When you tell him he needs to go to the hospital, he refuses, saying, 'I'm fine — I get these all the time. I'll just clean it up with some whiskey. You can't make me go anywhere.' The patient is alert enough to carry on a conversation but is clearly medically precarious with signs of severe infection and possible sepsis.
critical.question: This patient has a life-threatening wound infection with sepsis criteria (fever, tachycardia, confusion). He is homeless and refusing transport. How should the EMS provider manage this situation?
options: Honor his refusal since he is alert and conversational — have him sign an AMA form

Explain your specific concerns in plain language: 'This infection is spreading quickly up your leg and into your blood. Without treatment, you could die in the next 24 hours. The hospital can help — they will give you antibiotics through an IV and clean the wound properly.' Ask if there is anything you can do to make him more comfortable with the decision (call a friend, arrange for his belongings to be held). If he continues to refuse, involve law enforcement and medical direction. The patient's confusion (from sepsis) may impair his decision-making capacity, making his refusal invalid
Leave him at the encampment with a first aid kit and instructions to clean the wound
Tell him he has no choice — force him onto the stretcher and transport
